_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for Robeson County, NC. Record survival, courthouse history, and known losses. Special attention to the three-race record system and the unique challenges of Lumbee genealogical research — racial reclassification across censuses, separate Indian/white/Black record streams, and the absence of BIA records.
_dignity_mandate: The record challenges documented here reflect failures of classification systems, not ambiguity in Lumbee identity. Present record gaps and racial instability as institutional failures, not as evidence of uncertain ancestry.
display_name: Robeson County, North Carolina — Courthouse Atlas

current_courthouse.location: Lumberton, NC — Elm Street (historic courthouse, now supplemented by county administration buildings)

previous_courthouses.location: Lumberton, original structure on or near the current courthouse square
previous_courthouses.fate: Replaced by a larger structure as the county grew. The original building was frame or log construction typical of the era.
Replaced by the 1909 building. Some records were transferred; no documented fire or catastrophic loss during transition.

record_losses.cause: Civil War — Lowry War period disruption
records_destroyed: Some loose court papers and justice of the peace records from 1860s
record_losses.description: Robeson County escaped the major courthouse fires that destroyed records in some NC counties. The Lowry War period (1864-1872) created civil disruption but did not result in courthouse destruction. Some loose papers from the 1860s are missing, likely scattered during the political turmoil rather than deliberately destroyed. The bound volumes — deeds, wills, court minutes — survived intact. Robeson County's record survival is good by southeastern NC standards.
record_losses.narrative_impact: The courthouse survived, but the records it contains are complicated by the three-race classification system. {ancestor_name}'s records exist — the challenge is finding them across multiple racial filing systems, not replacing records that burned.
record_losses.source: NC State Archives. Guide to County Records in the North Carolina State Archives. Raleigh, 2001. Evans, William McKee. To Die Game. Syracuse: Syracuse University Press, 1995.

deeds.completeness: Good — continuous series from county formation. Lumbee land ownership is well-documented in deed books.
deeds.gaps: No known significant gaps. Some early deed books show wear but remain legible.
deeds.repository: Robeson County Register of Deeds, Lumberton; NC State Archives microfilm
deeds.online_access: FamilySearch microfilm; Ancestry.com NC land records collection (partial)
deeds.lumbee_research_note: Lumbee families appear in deed records as landowners from the earliest period. Land records are the MOST reliable source for tracking Lumbee families because they are not filtered through the racial classification instability that plagues census and vital records. A deed records a transaction — the race of the parties is not always noted, and when it is, it was less subject to enumerator discretion than census forms.

wills_estates.completeness: Good — wills and estate records survive from county formation
wills_estates.gaps: Some loose estate papers from the 1790s-1810s are fragmentary. Bound will books are intact.
wills_estates.repository: Robeson County Clerk of Court; NC State Archives
wills_estates.online_access: FamilySearch microfilm; NC State Archives digital collections

marriages.completeness: Good — marriage bonds survive from county formation. THREE SEPARATE STREAMS exist after 1885: white, Indian, and colored/Black.
marriages.gaps: Pre-1885 marriages were filed in two categories (white and colored/free colored). Lumbee marriages were filed in the 'colored' category before 1885, which does NOT indicate African ancestry in the Robeson County context. After 1885, separate Indian marriage records were established.
marriages.repository: Robeson County Register of Deeds; NC State Archives microfilm
marriages.online_access: FamilySearch; Ancestry.com NC marriages (may not distinguish between the three race-specific filing systems)
marriages.lumbee_research_note: CHECK ALL THREE FILING SYSTEMS for Lumbee marriages. Before 1885, check 'colored' records. After 1885, check Indian records first, then colored and white as a precaution. Automated record-matching systems on genealogy websites frequently fail to distinguish between the three streams.

court_minutes.completeness: Good — County Court minutes nearly continuous. Superior Court minutes from 1807.
court_minutes.gaps: Minor gaps in early records. The Lowry War period (1864-1872) generated extensive criminal court records that document Lumbee family names and community geography in unusual detail.
court_minutes.repository: NC State Archives; Robeson County Clerk of Court
court_minutes.online_access: NC State Archives microfilm; limited FamilySearch digitization
court_minutes.lumbee_research_note: Court minutes from the Lowry War period are a uniquely rich source for Lumbee families. Criminal proceedings, bounty records, and militia reports name dozens of Lumbee individuals and document community affiliations. The NC State Archives holds the state's Lowry Band files.

vital_records.completeness: Spotty until approximately 1920. Earlier vital events must be documented through church records, census data, and family Bible entries.
vital_records.repository: NC Office of Vital Records; NC State Archives for delayed birth certificates
vital_records.lumbee_research_note: Vital records classify individuals by race. Lumbee ancestors may appear as 'Indian,' 'Croatan,' 'Cherokee Indian of Robeson County,' or other designations depending on the era. Cross-reference with church and school records for identity confirmation.

indian_school_records.completeness: Variable — some Indian school enrollment records survive, particularly for the Pembroke Indian Normal School (est. 1887) and its successors.
indian_school_records.gaps: Elementary Indian school records are scattered across individual schools and the county school board. Not all survive.
indian_school_records.repository: UNC Pembroke Archives (normal school/college records); Robeson County school system records; NC State Archives
indian_school_records.online_access: Limited — UNC Pembroke archives have some finding aids online
indian_school_records.lumbee_research_note: Indian school enrollment is one of the strongest confirmation sources for Lumbee identity. Enrollment at an Indian school or at Pembroke Indian Normal School / Pembroke State College for Indians confirms community membership.

substitute_sources.description: Population schedules 1790-1940. Robeson County is enumerated from the first federal census. CAUTION: racial classification changes across censuses for Lumbee families (see hidden_history file for details). Match by name, age, household composition, and community location — NOT by racial designation.
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Essential but requires careful interpretation. The race column is the least reliable field for Lumbee families. Slave schedules (1850-1860) are relevant for white slaveholders in the county but do NOT apply to Lumbee families, who were free.
substitute_sources.source: National Archives and Records Administration. Federal Census Schedules, Robeson County, NC.

substitute_sources.description: Church records from the all-Indian Baptist association founded in 1880. Membership rolls, church minutes, letters of dismissal, and marriage records maintained by individual congregations within the Association.
substitute_sources.repository: Individual Burnt Swamp Association churches; Baptist Historical Collection, Wake Forest University; Lumbee Tribe Cultural Center
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: THE most reliable source for confirming Lumbee identity and tracing Lumbee family relationships. Church membership confirms community belonging. Letters of dismissal document migration. Church minutes record family events. These records reflect the community's own self-identification.
substitute_sources.source: Lowery, Malinda Maynor. Lumbee Indians in the Jim Crow South. Chapel Hill: UNC Press, 2010.

substitute_sources.description: Enrollment records, student files, and institutional documents from the Indian Normal School (1887), Pembroke State College for Indians (1941), and successor institutions.
substitute_sources.repository: UNC Pembroke Archives and Special Collections
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Enrollment at the Indian Normal School or Pembroke State College for Indians confirms Indian identity as recognized by the community and the state. Student files may contain hometown, parents' names, and demographic information.
substitute_sources.source: Dial, Adolph L., and David K. Eliades. The Only Land I Know. Syracuse: Syracuse University Press, 1996.

substitute_sources.source_type: Lowry War records — state militia and court files
substitute_sources.description: State militia expedition reports, bounty notices, court proceedings, and newspaper coverage related to Henry Berry Lowry's resistance (1864-1872).
substitute_sources.repository: NC State Archives (Lowry Band files); Robeson County courthouse (court proceedings); newspaper microfilm collections
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: These records name dozens of Lumbee families and document community affiliations, kinship networks, and settlement geography in unusual detail for a non-white community of this era. However, they were created by hostile authorities and must be read with that bias in mind.
substitute_sources.source: Evans, William McKee. To Die Game: The Story of the Lowry Band. Syracuse: Syracuse University Press, 1995.

narrative_hooks: The Robeson County courthouse survived — no fire, no flood, no army burned it. The records exist. The challenge for {ancestor_name}'s family is not missing records but misclassified ones: three separate filing systems for three races, and a census that changed what it called the Lumbee every decade.

If {ancestor_name} married in Robeson County before 1885, the marriage bond is filed under 'colored' — not because the family was of African descent, but because the courthouse had no 'Indian' category until the Croatan Act created one.

The deed books in the Robeson County vault run unbroken back to 1787, and {ancestor_name}'s land transactions are probably in them — the one record type where the racial designation does not matter, because a deed records a sale, not a race.

The Burnt Swamp Baptist Association records may be the most important genealogical source for {ancestor_name}'s family — not the courthouse, not the census, but the church that the Lumbee built for themselves.

During the Lowry War, the state's militia reports named {ancestor_name}'s neighbors, mapped the community's geography, and documented kinship connections that the census had never bothered to record. The enemy's paperwork became the family's genealogical evidence.
source_references: NC State Archives. Guide to County Records in the North Carolina State Archives. Raleigh: Division of Archives and History, 2001.
Evans, William McKee. To Die Game: The Story of the Lowry Band, Indian Guerrillas of Reconstruction. Syracuse: Syracuse University Press, 1995.
Lowery, Malinda Maynor. Lumbee Indians in the Jim Crow South. Chapel Hill: University of North Carolina Press, 2010.
Dial, Adolph L., and David K. Eliades. The Only Land I Know: A History of the Lumbee Indians. Syracuse: Syracuse University Press, 1996.
Blu, Karen I. The Lumbee Problem: The Making of an American Indian People. Lincoln: University of Nebraska Press, 2001.
